scope: Phase 1 trilingual content factory — pedagogical review of English side; ITパスポート exam prep for international learners

findings.issue: White Box Test 'Disadvantages' column reads 'Cannot find errors in the program.' This is factually inverted/wrong. White-box testing's actual disadvantage is that it does NOT verify whether the program satisfies the specification (spec compliance); it can still find code-level errors but not requirements-level errors. Learner who studies this will confidently choose the wrong answer on an exam item that asks 'why supplement white-box with black-box'.
findings.evidence_jp: プログラムの誤りを見つけられない
findings.fix_suggestion: Replace with: 'Cannot verify whether the program meets the specification (only verifies internal logic paths, not spec compliance).' This is also how 平成 IT Passport past papers phrase it.
findings.exam_impact: Direct exam-item misdirection. White-box vs. black-box is a recurring シラバス mid-area 6 topic.

findings.issue: Black Box Test disadvantage row reads: 'Since not all complex tests are performed, there is a possibility that low-frequency defects (Bug) may not be found.' The jp source itself is ungrammatical ('すべての複雑なテストしないので'); the en rendering preserves the broken grammar verbatim and the meaning is unclear. A learner cannot extract the intended teaching point ('cannot exhaustively test all internal paths, so rare-path bugs may slip through').
findings.evidence_en: "Since not all complex tests are performed, there is a possibility that low-frequency defects (Bug) may not be found"
findings.fix_suggestion: Rewrite as: 'Because it does not exercise every internal execution path, rarely triggered defects (bugs) may go undetected.' Also fix the singular/plural agreement: '(Bug)' → '(bugs)'.
findings.exam_impact: Loss of clarity on a high-frequency exam topic.

findings.issue: ISO 9002 is shown as 'Quality Management Systems – Guidelines for the Application of ISO 9001:2015.' That standard number is ISO/TS 9002, not ISO 9002 in the family sense; more importantly, ISO 9002:1994 was withdrawn in 2000 and is not commonly taught as part of the active ISO 9000 family. Either the jp source intends ISO/TS 9002:2016 (in which case the en should say so) or the row is incorrect. As written, an international learner gets a misleading mental model of the active ISO 9000 family (which in practice = 9000 / 9001 / 9004).
findings.evidence_en: "Quality Management Systems – Guidelines for the Application of ISO 9001:2015"
findings.fix_suggestion: Either label as 'ISO/TS 9002:2016 — Guidelines for the application of ISO 9001:2015' (precise) or add a note: 'Technical specification, not a certification standard.' Keep ISO 9001 as the only certification target — that part is correct.
findings.exam_impact: ITパスポート rarely asks 9002 details, but the row could mislead in a passing-mention question.

findings.issue: Entity p363::3 has type='term' but surface is a full English sentence ('When you specify an argument when calling a function, that argument is passed to the function.') and definition is another sentence explaining program output. This is not a term; it is body prose mis-tagged as a term entity. For an English learner looking up a vocabulary entry, the 'term' surface is unusable. Pedagogically this also creates two adjacent definitions of 'Argument' (p363::0 and p363::3) with overlapping coverage, which is confusing.
findings.fix_suggestion: Re-tag as 'note' or merge into the surrounding paragraph entity. If kept, the surface should be a noun phrase (e.g., 'Function call with argument passing').
findings.exam_impact: No direct exam impact, but harms self-study flow and glossary indexing.

findings.issue: 'Trademark Right' definition asserts it is 'the only intellectual property right whose validity period can be renewed.' This is a Japan-law-specific claim (商標法第19条 — renewable). In other jurisdictions (US, EU, CN) trademark rights are also renewable, so the 'only' claim is jurisdictionally correct within Japanese IP law context but reads as a global statement in English. International learners may dispute it.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add 'Under Japanese intellectual property law, …' as a leading clause, or footnote 'In the IT Passport context, this refers to Japanese law.'
findings.exam_impact: Exam-context fine, but learner trust suffers.

findings.issue: ISO 9000 definition reads: 'ISO 9000 is a standard concerning quality management systems. It is a standard for improving product quality.' Two sentences, both say the same thing. The second adds no information and is mildly circular ('standard ... for improving quality').
findings.fix_suggestion: Replace second sentence with: 'It defines fundamental concepts and vocabulary used across the ISO 9000 family.'
findings.exam_impact: Weak conceptual grounding for a foundational table.

findings.issue: Definition of VC: 'A company that invests in venture companies. "Venture" means "adventure" and "Capital" means "capital".' Telling an English-speaking learner that 'Capital' means 'capital' is tautological and noise. The jp original is teaching katakana etymology — but in en this is filler that should be cut or replaced.
findings.fix_suggestion: EN-only line: 'A firm that provides equity financing to venture (high-growth, high-risk) companies in exchange for an ownership stake.'
findings.exam_impact: Low; aesthetic/credibility issue.

findings.issue: M&A definition is just 'The act of acquiring or merging companies.' Misses the conceptual point that M is consolidating into one entity and A is one buying another — a frequent ITパスポート distractor. Also misses the 'why' (strategic growth lever).
findings.fix_suggestion: Expand: 'Mergers (combining two companies into one) and Acquisitions (one company buying another). Used as a fast-growth strategy versus organic growth.'
findings.exam_impact: Distractor 'M vs A' could trip learners.

findings.issue: Definition compresses 'AI / Generative AI / Deep Learning / Machine Learning' interrelationships into one sentence. Learner seeing 'Deep learning is used to build Generative AI' as a standalone term entry must hold all four concepts in mind. The table at p146::0 already lays out the hierarchy — but the term-level entry doesn't reference back.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add a one-line situating sentence: 'A subset of AI built on deep-learning (multi-layer neural network) models — see Machine Learning → Neural Network → Deep Learning above.'
findings.exam_impact: Conceptual ladder is needed for ITパスポート since each layer is tested separately.

findings.issue: Industry 4.0 definition lists 1st/2nd/3rd Industrial Revolutions (steam, electricity, computers) but never explicitly says 'Industry 4.0 = the fourth.' The English reader sees three revolutions then 'innovation in manufacturing using IoT and AI' without the binding 'this is the 4th revolution' framing. Easy to fix.
findings.fix_suggestion: Rewrite first sentence: 'The Fourth Industrial Revolution — innovation in manufacturing using IoT (Internet of Things) and AI (Artificial Intelligence). The First used steam engines, the Second electricity, the Third computers, and the Fourth (Industry 4.0) cyber-physical systems.'
findings.exam_impact: Frequent exam target; framing matters.

findings.issue: Definition says firmware 'is written as read-only to prevent users from accidentally overwriting it, and because it is not intended to be rewritten, it is also called firm (fixed) ware.' Modern firmware (UEFI, smartphone bootloaders, IoT devices) is routinely updated via firmware updates — strictly read-only is no longer accurate. The teaching simplification might cost learners on an exam item involving 'firmware update' scenarios.
findings.fix_suggestion: Soften: 'Originally written into ROM to prevent accidental overwriting; modern firmware can typically be updated (firmware update) but is still treated as semi-permanent.'
findings.exam_impact: ITパスポート does include 'firmware update' scenarios; current text undersells that aspect.

findings.issue: Cloud Computing's standalone term entry is defined entirely by contrast with Hosting Service ('Unlike Hosting Service, the location of the contracted server is unclear.'). A learner who encounters Cloud first (without context for Hosting) cannot understand it from this entry alone. Also the canonical NIST-style 'on-demand, scalable, pay-per-use' framing is missing.
findings.fix_suggestion: Lead with: 'A service model in which computing resources (servers, storage, applications) are provided on demand over the Internet, typically priced by usage. Unlike hosting services, the physical location of the underlying server is abstracted and may be unknown to the customer.'
findings.exam_impact: Cloud is heavily tested; weak primary definition is a real risk.

findings.issue: Definition spends two clauses on the etymology of 'Literacy' ('originally means the ability to read and write'). For an English-speaking learner that is self-evident and uses precious cognitive bandwidth that could go to ITパスポート-specific framing.
findings.fix_suggestion: EN-only: 'The ability to gather, evaluate, use, and disseminate information — including the practical use of IT tools (computers, the Internet) to do so.'
findings.exam_impact: Low.

findings.issue: 'Green Purchasing Law' definition does not flag that this is a **specifically Japanese statute** (グリーン購入法, formally Act on Promoting Green Procurement, 2001). An English-speaking learner may mistake this for a general principle or a non-Japanese law.
findings.fix_suggestion: Lead with: 'A Japanese law (Act on Promoting Green Procurement, 2001) that requires national government bodies to prioritize purchasing environmentally friendly products …'
findings.exam_impact: Same risk as the Personal Information Protection Act / GDPR confusion called out in the agent spec.

findings.issue: RFP defined as 'A document created by the ordering company and handed to the vendor.' True but content-free — doesn't say what the document contains (functional requirements, evaluation criteria, schedule, budget envelope). Compare with the much fuller p225::3 (RFI) definition just two entries down.
findings.fix_suggestion: Expand: 'A document the ordering company sends to candidate vendors describing the system to be built, functional/non-functional requirements, evaluation criteria, schedule, and budget envelope — and asking each vendor to submit a Proposal.'
findings.exam_impact: Moderate; ITパスポート tests the RFI→RFP→Proposal→Contract→Acceptance flow.

findings.issue: Term entity surface is a full English sentence 'The Boundaries That Distinguish Computers Are Becoming Blurry.' Like R18-004, this should be a noun phrase or be re-typed as a 'note'/'paragraph' entity. Glossary indexing breaks if such sentences enter the term index.
findings.fix_suggestion: Either re-tag (preferred) or use the noun-phrase form 'Blurring boundaries between computer types.'
findings.exam_impact: No direct exam impact.

findings.issue: IMAP definition includes meta-commentary about the exam: 'In the IT Passport exam, it is sometimes asked with the phrase …' This is helpful exam-prep context, but in a content-factory term entry, mixing definition with exam wording trivia can confuse a non-Japanese learner reading the en side as a glossary entry.
findings.fix_suggestion: Move the exam phrasing note to a separate 'exam_note' field or a parenthetical: '(Exam wording variant: a protocol for viewing email managed on a mail server.)'
findings.exam_impact: Actually helpful for exam — categorize as INFO not WARN.

findings.issue: Question on the most reliable method to prevent HDD information leakage. Answer index 0 (overwrite multiple times) is correct, but the page entity provides no explanation. International learners may not know that physical destruction is even more reliable, or why logical format / recycle bin / password lock are insufficient. Other question pages also lack rationale, but this one is a high-stakes security topic.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add an 'explanation' field per question entity with: 'B: logical format preserves recoverable data. C: deleted files are recoverable until overwritten. D: password lock can be bypassed by removing the disk. A: overwriting with random data is the standard secure-erase method (US DoD 5220.22-M pattern).'
findings.exam_impact: Significant; questions without explanations have lower study value.

findings.issue: Question asks for an appropriate example of Freemium. answer_index=2 picks option C (business card service, free basic + paid premium). C correctly matches the freemium model (free tier + paid premium features). PASS. Just flagging that A/B/D are correctly distinguished (A=flat-rate, B=freeware, D=ad-supported free distribution) — these are NOT freemium.
findings.fix_suggestion: Same as R18-018 — add an explanation per choice. INFO only.
findings.exam_impact: Already correct; explanation would lift study value.

findings.issue: ICANN is described as managing IP addresses 'so they do not duplicate worldwide.' ICANN's actual primary role is coordination of the DNS root and assignment of global unique identifiers (IP address blocks are assigned by IANA — operated under ICANN — and distributed via RIRs like ARIN/APNIC/RIPE/LACNIC/AfriNIC). A more precise English wording matters because ITパスポート has begun asking about IANA / RIR layer.
findings.fix_suggestion: Rephrase: 'A nonprofit organization (headquartered in the United States) that coordinates the global Domain Name System (DNS) and, through its IANA function, the allocation of IP address blocks to regional Internet registries.'
findings.exam_impact: Light but real.

findings.issue: Definition says 'It is protected under the Patent Act' — singular 'the Patent Act' is unattributed. International learners may not know which jurisdiction's Patent Act. Implicitly Japan (特許法), but a learner from US/EU/CN/IN reads ambiguously.
findings.fix_suggestion: 'Protected under the Japanese Patent Act (特許法)' or 'patent law (in Japan, the Patent Act 特許法).'
findings.exam_impact: Low.

findings.issue: Figure caption 'Even managers want to take it easy!' is colloquial and may strike international/business learners as oddly informal in a certification-prep text. The jp original (経営者だって楽をしたい！) is equally informal but reads as light commentary in Japanese textbook culture; in English it can come across unprofessional.
findings.fix_suggestion: Soften: 'Managers, too, want their work made easier.' or 'Even executives appreciate efficiency tools.'
findings.exam_impact: None.

findings.issue: Definition is just 'Japanese name for the AND operation.' For the en side, this is content-free — an English-speaking learner already knows AND. Same applies to p327::1 (Logical OR), p327::2 (XOR), p327::3 (Negation). These four entries on the en side provide zero pedagogical value beyond pointing to the Japanese name (which is irrelevant on the en side).
findings.fix_suggestion: Either suppress these four entries on the en surface or replace definition.en with the actual truth-table semantics, e.g. 'Logical AND: result is 1 (true) only when all inputs are 1; otherwise 0. Japanese: 論理積.'
findings.exam_impact: Foundational but learner is unlikely to be stuck on AND/OR/NOT without seeing the truth tables; tables p327::6/p327::7 are correct.

findings.issue: IPO definition: 'The act of a company listing on the stock market and making its previously unlisted shares publicly available.' 'Stock market' should be 'stock exchange'; 'making shares publicly available' should be 'offering shares to the public for the first time.'
findings.fix_suggestion: 'A company's first sale of its shares to the public (and listing on a stock exchange), allowing previously private equity to be traded publicly.'
findings.exam_impact: Minor wording.

findings.issue: Alliance: 'The act of forming partnerships between companies.' Tautological — partnership ≈ alliance. Doesn't explain what makes a strategic alliance distinct (capital tie-up, joint R&D, marketing tie-up without merger).
findings.fix_suggestion: 'A cooperative agreement between independent companies — for example joint R&D, capital tie-up, or marketing partnership — without merging into a single entity (contrast with M&A).'
findings.exam_impact: Low.

positive_observations: Page 199 (Cloud Computing 4-panel figure with on-prem/housing/hosting/cloud) is excellently structured for English learners: each panel carries its own location/server/notes fields. Great pedagogical scaffolding.
Page 146 (AI/ML/Deep Learning/Generative AI) table establishes the conceptual hierarchy clearly on the en side.
Page 327 truth tables (NAND, NOR) render correctly on the en side.
Page 432 (Differential vs Incremental Backup) — definitions are crisp, contrast is sharp, and they teach the key conceptual distinction in one sentence each.
Page 555 questions use idiomatic English ('eavesdropping by intercepting radio waves between the PC and the access point') — natural for international learners.
Page 119 IPO/M&A/Alliance/Joint Venture cluster covers a coherent topic area in a compact glossary block — structurally sound.
global_observations: EN side is overall fluent and idiomatic — no machine-translation artifacts at the sentence level except R18-002 (which inherits broken grammar from the jp source).
Most term entities have appropriate noun-phrase surfaces; exceptions are R18-004 and R18-016 (full sentences mis-tagged as terms).

Question entities (181, 300, 555) have NO 'explanation' field. For an exam-prep product targeting international learners, the absence of per-choice rationales is a system-level pedagogical gap (logged once as R18-018, but applies broadly).
Acronyms (ISO, IoT, AI, RFP, RFI, SLA, BSC, VC, IPO, M&A, POP, IMAP, ICANN, DBMS, ROM, ESSID, WPA2, MAC, FAQ) are consistently expanded in en — good practice for international learners.

Jurisdiction signaling for Japan-specific concepts (Patent Act, Green Purchasing Law, Trademark Right) is inconsistent — sometimes absent. Recommend a global policy: any en definition referencing a Japanese statute should lead with 'Under Japanese law,' or include a (Japan) tag.
verdict_rationale: 4 FAIL findings — most severe is R18-001/R18-002 (inverted white-box advantage/disadvantage on page 249, a high-frequency ITパスポート exam topic). R18-003 (ISO 9002 imprecision) and R18-004 (schema misuse) are also blocking for a polished release. WARN-level findings (12) cluster around jurisdiction context for Japan-specific laws and thin/tautological definitions for foundational concepts. Recommend fixing 4 FAILs + jurisdiction WARNs (R18-005, R18-014, R18-021) before release.
